content.text: Agno — formerly known as Phidata — is a Python-first framework for building production-ready AI agents. It wraps multiple LLM providers behind a single
class, adds tool registration, long-term memory via pluggable storage backends, RAG-ready knowledge bases, and a multi-agent
abstraction that handles delegation and coordination.

Unlike heavier orchestration frameworks, Agno prioritises fast iteration: an agent with memory and tools can be running in under twenty lines of code, while the same primitives scale to complex multi-agent pipelines.

attrs.alt: Agno agent architecture: Agent connecting to Model, Tools, Knowledge, Storage, and Team

content.text: Agno is distributed on PyPI. Install the core package and any model provider SDK you plan to use:
extra. The framework supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Mistral, Cohere, AWS Bedrock, Groq, and local models via Ollama.
Every Agno program starts with an
instance. At minimum you supply a model and optional instructions:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat

agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    instructions="You are a helpful assistant.",
    markdown=True,
)

agent.print_response("What is the capital of France?")
sends the prompt, streams the reply to stdout with markdown rendering, and returns an
to get the response object instead of printing.
and Agno will auto-generate a JSON schema from the type hints and docstring, register it with the model, and call it whenever the model decides:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.tools import tool

@tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Return the current weather for a city."""
    # real implementation would call a weather API
    return f"Sunny, 22°C in {city}"

agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    tools=[get_weather],
    instructions="Use tools to answer questions accurately.",
)

agent.print_response("What's the weather in Tokyo?")
Agno converts type hints and docstrings into the tool schema automatically. Always annotate parameters and write a clear one-line docstring — these become the tool description the model reads.
Agno ships dozens of pre-built toolkits for common integrations. Pass toolkit instances directly to the

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.tools.duckduckgo import DuckDuckGoTools
from agno.tools.yfinance import YFinanceTools

agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    tools=[DuckDuckGoTools(), YFinanceTools(stock_price=True)],
    show_tool_calls=True,
)

agent.print_response("What is AAPL's current price and latest news?")
(persisting conversation history across sessions) from in-context memory.
writes messages to a local SQLite file;
targets a Postgres instance for multi-process deployments:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.storage.sqlite import SqliteStorage

agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    storage=SqliteStorage(table_name="my_agent", db_file="agent.db"),
    # Pass a session_id to resume an existing conversation
    # session_id="abc-123",
    add_history_to_messages=True,
)

# First interaction
agent.print_response("My name is Alice.")

# Second interaction — the agent remembers Alice
agent.print_response("What is my name?")
to automatically inject the session history into every subsequent request. Pass a fixed
to resume a specific conversation rather than starting a new one.
Agno's knowledge layer handles document ingestion, chunking, embedding, and retrieval. The vector database sits behind an abstract interface so you can swap backends without changing agent code:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.knowledge.pdf import PDFKnowledgeBase
from agno.vectordb.pgvector import PgVector

kb = PDFKnowledgeBase(
    path="docs/manual.pdf",
    vector_db=PgVector(
        table_name="manual_chunks",
        db_url="postgresql://localhost/agno",
    ),
)
kb.load()  # splits, embeds, and stores chunks

agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    knowledge=kb,
    search_knowledge=True,
)

agent.print_response("Summarise the warranty section.")
once to ingest and index documents. Subsequent agent runs query the index automatically when
and Agno ensures the reply is parsed and validated before returning. This eliminates brittle string parsing of JSON outputs:

from pydantic import BaseModel
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat

class MovieReview(BaseModel):
    title: str
    rating: float
    summary: str
    pros: list[str]
    cons: list[str]

agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    response_model=MovieReview,
)

result = agent.run("Review the movie Inception.")
review: MovieReview = result.content
print(review.title, review.rating)
— fully typed with IDE autocompletion and runtime validation. If the model's output does not match the schema, Agno retries automatically.
setting assigns a leader model that plans tasks and delegates to specialist members;
routes messages to the single best member;
lets all members contribute and synthesises their outputs:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.team import Team

researcher = Agent(
    name="Researcher",
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    instructions="Search for information and summarise findings.",
)

writer = Agent(
    name="Writer",
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    instructions="Take research notes and write a polished article.",
)

team = Team(
    members=[researcher, writer],
    mode="coordinate",          # leader plans and delegates
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
)

team.print_response("Write an article about recent advances in fusion energy.")
Leader plans sub-tasks and delegates to members in sequence
Leader selects the single best-fit member for each request
All members run concurrently; outputs are merged
adds a step where the model explicitly reasons through a problem before producing a final answer. It is well-suited to math, logic, and multi-step analysis tasks:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.reasoning import ReasoningAgent

agent = ReasoningAgent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="o3-mini"),
    instructions="Think step by step before answering.",
)

agent.print_response("Prove that the square root of 2 is irrational.")
Agno supports both native chain-of-thought models (OpenAI o3-mini, o1) and simulates reasoning on standard chat models by prompting them to think step-by-step before answering.
on the agent or on individual calls to iterate over response chunks as they arrive. This is essential for low-latency user interfaces:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat

agent = Agent(model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"), stream=True)

for chunk in agent.run("Tell me a short story.", stream=True):
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)
carries the incremented text. When tool calls appear mid-stream the framework buffers them, executes the tools, and continues streaming the final reply.
Comparing Agno to Other Agent Frameworks
Re-instantiating agents per request loses session history. Create agents once and reuse them, using storage to persist state across process restarts.
re-ingests and re-embeds all documents on every call. Call it once at startup, or add a guard like
decorator infers the schema from the function signature. Missing type hints or vague docstrings cause the model to misuse or skip the tool.
mode add an extra LLM call (the leader's planning step). For latency-sensitive tasks, prefer
mode or a single agent with multiple tools.
Agno ships a local web UI called the Playground that lets you interact with agents in a browser. It is ideal for quick demonstrations and debugging tool calls without writing a frontend:

from agno.playground import Playground, serve_playground_app
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat

agent = Agent(
    name="Demo Agent",
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    instructions="You are a helpful assistant.",
)

app = Playground(agents=[agent]).get_app()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    serve_playground_app("main:app", reload=True)
to get a chat interface. Each agent registered with
appears as a separate tab. Tool calls and their arguments are shown inline in the conversation.
Production applications typically need multiple concurrent sessions — one per user. The recommended pattern creates a new agent instance per session, loading history from storage:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.storage.sqlite import SqliteStorage
import uuid

def create_session_agent(session_id: str | None = None) -> Agent:
    return Agent(
        model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
        storage=SqliteStorage(table_name="sessions", db_file="sessions.db"),
        session_id=session_id or str(uuid.uuid4()),
        add_history_to_messages=True,
        num_history_responses=10,   # keep last 10 exchanges
    )

# Start a new session
agent = create_session_agent()
print("Session ID:", agent.session_id)
agent.print_response("Hello, I am Alice.")

# Resume the same session later
resumed = create_session_agent(session_id=agent.session_id)
resumed.print_response("What is my name?")
caps how many past exchanges are injected into the context window, preventing unbounded token growth in long-running sessions. For applications where memory must survive process restarts,
alongside user data in your application database. It is the only key that links a user to their conversation history.
Agno supports AWS Bedrock as a first-class provider. Swap
to route calls through Bedrock with your existing AWS credentials:

from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.aws import Claude as BedrockClaude
from agno.tools.duckduckgo import DuckDuckGoTools

agent = Agent(
    model=BedrockClaude(id="anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0"),
    tools=[DuckDuckGoTools()],
    instructions="Use web search to answer factual questions.",
)

agent.print_response("What are the latest AI model releases?")
The Bedrock model class accepts any Anthropic model ID available on Bedrock. Credentials are read from the standard AWS credential chain (environment variables,
, or an IAM role). No Agno-specific configuration is needed beyond the model ID.
is the core primitive — compose it with a model, tools, storage, and knowledge.
registers Python functions; type hints and docstrings become the schema the model reads.
Built-in toolkits (DuckDuckGo, YFinance, SQLTools, etc.) cover common integrations with a single import.
persist conversation history across sessions automatically.
and friends handle chunking, embedding, and retrieval; set
with a Pydantic class gives fully validated, typed structured outputs without manual parsing.
orchestrates multi-agent workflows in coordinate, route, or collaborate mode.
Agno is optimised for fast iteration on single-file scripts while scaling to production multi-agent deployments.
